coredata.dc:title: Development of modified polymer dot as stimuli-sensitive and 67Ga radio-carrier, for investigation of in vitro drug delivery, in vivo imaging and drug release kinetic
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Kashani, Hediyeh Mahmood
Madrakian, Tayyebeh
Afkhami, Abbas
coredata.dc:description: A polymer dot modified histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot carrier, PD@His.GQD, was synthesized to investigate the in vitro sunitinib (STB) deliveryvia luminescence spectrometer. The carrier’s synthesis, with an average size of 34 nm, was proved by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analyses. In the in vitro, STB delivery investigation showed that for healthy tissue, the STB was loaded at pH = 7.2 and at 25 = 5.4 at 37 °C with a maximum loading efficiency percentage of 99 % while it was released at pH = 5.4 at 37 °C with a release percentage of 97 %. In the sequel, the STB loaded carrier was labeled with Gallium-67 (67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD) to produce exceedingly transparent radio-carrier for in vivo kidney cancerous mice imaging via the single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) device. The radiochemical purity of the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD was obtained as 95 % by Radio Thin Layer Chromatography (RTLC) and High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) analysis. All obtained results affirmed that the synthesized PD@His.GQD is an STB stimuli-sensitive and selective targeting carrier. All cancerous mice in vivo images at 10 and 20 h of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD post-injection and its bio-distribution calculations showed its most accumulation in the kidney cancerous tissue. Also, the STB release kinetic was studied via Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, and the release data were fitted with Korsmeyer-Peppas model that expresses the STB release mechanism is controlled by diffusion.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A polymer dot modified histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot carrier, PD@His.GQD, was synthesized to investigate the in vitro sunitinib (STB) deliveryvia luminescence spectrometer. The carrier’s synthesis, with an average size of 34 nm, was proved by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analyses. In the in vitro, STB delivery investigation showed that for healthy tissue, the STB was loaded at pH = 7.2 and at 25 = 5.4 at 37 °C with a maximum loading efficiency percentage of 99 % while it was released at pH = 5.4 at 37 °C with a release percentage of 97 %. In the sequel, the STB loaded carrier was labeled with Gallium-67 (67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD) to produce exceedingly transparent radio-carrier for in vivo kidney cancerous mice imaging via the single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) device. The radiochemical purity of the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD was obtained as 95 % by Radio Thin Layer Chromatography (RTLC) and High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) analysis. All obtained results affirmed that the synthesized PD@His.GQD is an STB stimuli-sensitive and selective targeting carrier. All cancerous mice in vivo images at 10 and 20 h of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD post-injection and its bio-distribution calculations showed its most accumulation in the kidney cancerous tissue. Also, the STB release kinetic was studied via Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, and the release data were fitted with Korsmeyer-Peppas model that expresses the STB release mechanism is controlled by diffusion.

ce:keyword.ce:text: In vivo imaging of kidney cancerous mice

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer remains as one of the world’s most devastating diseases, with more than 10 million new cases every year and one of the leading causes of death in most parts of the world. Kidney cancer is a type of cancer that it is about 2.3 % of all kind of cancers in the world, which makes it one of the 100 most common cancers in the world [1]. Therefore, targeting or smart drug delivery systems, also known as "magic bullets", were introduced in the mid-1980s as highly efficient therapeutic systems that can selectively target the kidney cancerous tissues. Also, these systems sustain the targeting disease tissues required drug level without any substantial toxic effect on healthy cells, thus eliminating the drugs toxicity and killing disease cells [2].

Newly, variant drug delivery systems like nanoparticles and quantum dots have been expanded as effectual carriers [3–6]. Among these drug carriers, fluorescent quantum dot (QD) carriers with small size, has been highly regarded for the synthesis of drug carriers compared to nano-drug carriers due to their impressive properties such as: 1) zero-dimensional structure, 2) high drug loading efficiency, 3) appropriate preparation and purification method 4) great biocompatibility/low toxicity, 5) certain mechanical strength and stability, 6) small particle size and appropriate shape, 7) facile surface functionalization, 8) excellent photo-stability and 9) longer residence in vivo time [7]. Among these fluorescent quantum dot carriers, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that were discovered by Ponomarenko in 2008 due to these impressive features are a worthy candidate in the drug delivery systems synthesis that their surface modification with amine compounds is a very effective method to diminish the size, enhance fluorescence intensity/quantum yield and raise the water-solubility of them [8]. With the goal of designing a targeting carrier for killing kidney cancerous cells, histidine (His) amino acid was conjugated to the GQDs. The reason of this conjunction was that according to the previously obtained results from western blot analysis and reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction, HSP90 proteins are on the kidney cancerous cells that contain histidine amino acid [1,9].

Polymer dots (PDs) that possess both the QDs fluorescent properties and the performance of the polymers, have recently emerged as a new class of materials in the fields of in vivo imaging, drug/gene delivery, and biological sensors, owing to their high fluorescence emission /quantum yields, non-blinking, great biocompatibility and easily surfaces modification [10]. Polymer dots (PDs) that synthesized from linear polymeric strings or monomers are dissected into conjugated and non-conjugated PDs. Because of the excellent water-solubility and environmentally friendly preparation methods of non-conjugated PDs (condensation, crosslinking, or slightly carbonization), these are used more than conjugated PDs to prevent consumption of organic solvents [11]. For widespread biomedical applications and improving the PD targeting efficiency, is required to functionalize the PD surface.

Sunitinib (STB) has been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration since January 2006 for treatment of kidney cancer as a small multi-targeted receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) inhibitor molecule and an oral anticancer drug. However, STB has different toxicities: impaired muscle coordination, head shakes, hypo-activity, ocular discharge, piloerection, and gastrointestinal distress [12]. Therefore the use of targeting drug delivery methods, instead of conventional chemotherapy, and selectively delivering the STB to the tumor tissue, in addition to eliminate the side effects of the drug, can prevent the tumor re-growth.

Different radioisotopes have been used for in vivo imaging, chiefly tumor imaging; among these, gallium-67 (67Ga) is most widely used in the radiopharmaceutical fields due to its unique features like much less toxic than other radioisotopes, transparency, 78-hs physical half-life, 36-hs biological half-life with no harm to the patient’s body and its ability to emit gamma ray and then record images via single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) device [13]. In this project, PD was modified with histidine functionalized GQD (PD@His.GQD) to synthesize a novel targeting drug delivery system and heighten drug loading efficiency, fluorescence intensity/quantum yield and to investigate the in vitro sunitinib loading/release conditions. In the sequel, for in vivo imaging, the synthesized carrier was loaded with STB (STB.PD@His.GQD) and then was labeled with 67Ga radioisotope (67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD) to develop a radio-carrier. This synthesized radio-carrier can reveal the size of kidney cancerous tissue and assess the efficacy of selective STB delivery/release to that with extremely transparent images.

Finally, when a new carrier is synthesized, it is very momentous the drug release kinetic studies using mathematical models. These models can predict in vivo drug release performance through the quantitative analysis of the in vitro drug release data for designing a superb controlled drug release systems. Therefore in this work to study the STB release kinetic and predict its release mechanism in the kidney cancerous tissue and thus achieving the best performance of the synthesized carrier, the lab obtained in vitro STB release data, fitted with the four kinetic models: Zero order model, First order model, Higuchi model, and Korsmeyer-Peppas [14].

In this study, for the first time, the PD@His.GQD and 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD were synthesized through one-pot and low-cost green methods for in vitro investigating the STB loading chemical conditions in the laboratory by luminescence spectrometry and in vivo imaging in a kidney cancerous mice model via SPECT device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials and chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical materials such as citric acid (CA, 99.5 %, Merck Company) as GQD carbon source, histidine amino acid (His, ≥99 %, Merck Company) for surface passivation of GQD, polyacrylic acid (PAA, MW = 3500 g mol−1, Sigma-Aldrich Company) as a source of polymer dot and ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich Company) as crosslinking agent were the main-drug carrier materials. The anhydrous methanol (≥99.8 %, Sigma-Aldrich Company) and anhydrous acetonitrile (≥99.8 %, Sigma-Aldrich Company), were used as solvents for radio thin layer chromatography (RTLC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The enriched zinc-68 chloride (≥95 %) was gained from ion beam separation group at Agricultural, Medical, and Industrial Research School (AMIRS) to generate gallium-67 at the 30 MeV Cyclotron. The quinine sulfate (98 MeV Cyclotron. The quinine sulfate (≥98 %, Sigma-Aldrich Company) was selected as the standard for calculating synthesized PD@His.GQD quantum yield. The sunitinib (STB, ≥98 %, Sigma-Aldrich Company) was used as a drug model for in vitro and in vivo drug delivery studies. The hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %, Merck Company), sodium acetate (CH3COONa, Sigma-Aldrich Company, 99 %) and acetic acid (CH3COOH, Sigma-Aldrich Company, 99 %) for acetate buffer (0.01 M, pH = M, pH = 5.4), sodium phosphate dibasic heptahydrate (Na2HPO4⋅7H2O, Sigma-Aldrich Company, ≥99 %) and sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate (NaH2PO4⋅H2O, Sigma-Aldrich Company, ≥99 %) for phosphate buffer (PB, 0.01 M, pH = 7.2) and Britton–Robinson buffer were utilize as raw materials without further purification. Deionized water (DW) was used regularly throughout the procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diagnostic instrumentation
ce:section.ce:para: The characterizations of synthesized PD@His.GQD carrier were accomplished using the following apparatus: to measure and check the fluorescence spectra of drug loading and release process, a Perkin-Elmer luminescence spectrometer (model LS-50B, 3.5 cm × 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cell, scan speed: 1200 nm min−1 and the band-slits of both excitation and emission: 10.0 nm) was applied. To measure the quantum yield and record the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PD@His.GQD, an agilent UV–vis spectrophotometer (model 8453, 3.5 cm × 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cell) was used. The pH adjustment of all solutions was accomplished by a digital Metrohm pH-meter model 731, equipped with a combined glass calomel electrode. The novel synthesized PD@His.GQD carrier was studied in terms of the nanostructure, size, and morphology via Philips-CM30 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) by exerting an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. To record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in the range of 4000−400 cm−1, a Perkin-Elmer GX FTIR spectrometer was used. To obtain the average hydrodynamic diameter and size distribution of the PD@His.GQD in the solution, the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurement was applied (model Nano ZS ZEN 3600, manufacturing of the Malvern Company in the UK, 3.5 cm × 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm quartz cell, using a 660 nm Vis-light).

To prove that the PD@His.GQD carrier is labeled with gallium-67 (67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD) and designate its specific radioactivity, High-performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) was accomplished via a Shimadzu LC-10AT device (Whatman Partisphere C-18 column 4.6 × 250 mm, Whatman, NJ, USA), equipped with a Packard-150 TR gamma detector. Radio Thin Layer Chromatography (RTLC) technique via Whatman No.1 papers, was used to determine the radiochemical purity, by a thin layer chromatography scanner, bioscan AR2000, bioscan europe ltd., Paris, France device. The biodistribution information was collected by counting the washed tissues with normal saline after weighing on GC1020-7500SL CanberraTM high purity germanium (HPGe) detector. After the injection of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD into the mice model, the in vivo images were captured at 2.0, 10, and 20 h via a Dual-Head SPECT imaging device (equipped with gamma camera). The method of working with laboratory animals was based on the United Kingdom Biological Council’s Guidelines of working with the Living Animals in Scientific Investigations [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green and cost-effective synthesis of His.GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The citric acid (CA) plays an essential role in energy production in healthy body and also in biochemistry as it is one of the vital stages of the metabolism process. According to the mentioned contents, citric acid is a green carbon source for graphene quantum dot synthesis. Furthermore, histidine (His) is also one of the twenty essential amino acids in the living cells and as mentioned above, HSP90 proteins on the kidney cancerous cells contain His amino acid. So, in addition to the consumed raw materials which are hundred percent green and essential for the body, the applied pyrolysis method is also a green method that no organic solvents and exorbitant/ laborious laboratory appurtenances have been used. In a typical pyrolysis synthesis, 2.0 g of CA and 0.50 g of His were added to a preheated 10 mL beaker (Temp = 160 °C). The admixture of CA and His were liquated, subsequently after 20 min the colorless liquid was turned into an orange solid that indicates the formation of GQD. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained orange solid was dissolved in 5.0 mL of DW. The synthesized His.GQD solution was then neutralized to pH = 7, by 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH and dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 3 kDa cut-off molecular weight. Because of incompletely carbonization of CA during the temperature/time controlled pyrolysis conditions and its surface-passivation with histidine imidazole rings, the synthesized His.GQD shows good ﬂuorescence intensity (FI) (Scheme 1 ). Fluorescence spectrum, FTIR analysis, XRD pattern and HR-TEM image were used to prove the synthesis of His.GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-step provision of PD@His.GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize a new targeting drug delivery system that has an extraordinary drug loading capacity, polymer dot (PD) was modified with the above synthesized His.GQD as PD@His.GQD, via an environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermally crosslinking procedure (non-conjugated strategy). The most important point in this synthesis strategy is not to use organic solvents so as a result it is non-toxic and highly useful to exploit the bio-based applications. This PD@His.GQD carrier has remarkable selectivity to kidney cancerous cells, extremely high FI/quantum yield and surface area. For this purpose, at first 278 μL of the His.GQD (pH = 7) was mixed with 40.0 mL aqueous solution containing 100 mg of polyacrylic acid (PAA) and 170 μL of ethylenediamine (EDA) and then stirred for 40 min. Afterward this yellow solution was transferred into a 50.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at optimized temperature of 200 ͦ C for 10 h in the oven. Amount of all-consuming materials, temperature and time were optimized to achieve the highest yield of carrier with the highest fluorescence intensity. After cooling the autoclave, the brown light product was dialyzed in a 3 kDa dialysis bag (Scheme 2 ) in order to remove the small molecules. To confirm the synthesis of PD@His.GQD carrier, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, FTIR analysis, DLS and TEM image were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ga-67 production and synthesis of 67Ga-STB. PD@His.GQD radio-carrier
ce:section.ce:para: For producing Ga-67 in form of GaCl3, the Zn-68 (p, 2n) Ga-67 target was regularly bombarded via 25.00 MeV, current intensity 100.0 μA and 1000 μA h at Cyclone-30. According to the latest optimized reported radiochemical separation methods, the product impurities were eliminated and ultimately the pure GaCl3 was obtained [13]. It is necessary to mention that 67Ga is produced as a medicinal radioisotope via radionuclide and chemical purity of over 99.9 %, also all sterilization processes are performed on it, so it has no toxicity for injection into the body. Afterward, 1.0 mL of the obtained 67GaCl3 (37.00 MBq, 1.000 mCi) was dried with the flow of N2 gas at 50 °C in a borosilicate vial, and then adjusted its pH at 7.2 via Britton-Robinson buffer (400.0 μL). To synthesize the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radio-carrier, at first 1.0 mL of the PD@His.GQD was loaded with 0.5 mL of 600.0 μg mL−1 STB, hereafter in a vial, 100.0 μL of synthesized STB.PD@His.GQD carrier was added to the gallium solution and stirred slowly at 25 °C for 40 min and at the end filtered with 0.22 μm filter. The radiochemical purity of the synthesize 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD was obtained as 95 % by RTLC and HPLC under optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield (QYs) computing
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of the quantum dots depend on their environment. On the other hand, an excited state in the quantum dots is formed when they absorb light photons, and then different quantum dots loss various numbers of these absorbed photons, depending on their inherent nature [16]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) determines the molecular brightness (MB) of quantum dots that is proportional to the number of emitted photons into the absorbed photons by a quantum dot. For these reasons, to calculate the QY, both the absorption (at the excitation wavelength) and fluorescence emission spectra are required. To compute the exact QY of the quantum dots and eliminate the inherent problems and errors in calculating the exact number of photons, a reference sample with a well-defined QY is used. To calculate the synthesized PD@His.GQDs quantum yield, the following formula was used [16]:
(1)
Q x
%
=
Q R
×
I x
I R
×
O D
R
O D
x

n x 2
n R 2
× 100
In this equation ”Q” is the QY, ”I” is the emission intensity calculated integral, ”n” is the refractive index of solvents, and ”OD” is the reference and PD@His.GQD samples optical density that is measured using UV–vis spectrophotometer at λex = 380 nm that should be less than 0.1 in order to solve the reabsorption problem [16]. The subscript ”R” and ”x” represent the reference (with known QY) and PD@His.GQD samples. In this research, the quinine sulfate was selected as a reference and the QY of the PD@His.GQD was calculated as 82.3 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesized targeting drug delivery system analysis
Fluorescence, UV–vis spectra and excitation-independent investigation of PD@His.GQD
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the PD@His.GQD drug carrier was synthesized and neutralized to pH = 7 in the phosphate buffer (PB) after dialyzing in dialysis bag, and then a suitable concentration of that was prepared. At first, the optical analysis of the PD@His.GQD were characterized by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1 a–c). As shown in Fig. 1a, one manifest absorption peak appeared at 332 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum that mainly attributed to the n−π* transition of COOH and CO bonds and proved the existence of these functional groups on the synthesized carrier. Afterward, the PD@His.GQD fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded at wavelengths 380 and 454 nm via a spectrofluorometer (Fig. 1b and c) with high fluorescence intensity of 875. To further peruse the PD@His.GQD optical properties, its excitation wavelength was changed in the range of λex = 360−450 nm (Fig. 1d). As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence emission of synthesized PD@His.GQD drug delivery system is excitation-independent that attests the carrier has dots with relatively uniform morphology with stable ground state and also its fluorescence properties are depending on the surface states rather than the different its sizes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total procedure
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vitro STB delivery, PD@His.GQD with FI = 875 at λex = 380 nm was synthesized as a smart drug delivery system. The obtained in vitro STB loading data that was perused by carrier FI variation at STB concentration range (CONC) 5.000−3000 μg mL−1 showed well that PD@His.GQD has high STB loading efficiency percentage of 99 % and release percentage of 97 %. Subsequently, the obtained in vivo images from one kidney cancerous mice model using SPECT device by the synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radio-carrier proved the selective and temperature (Temp)/pH sensitive performance of designed carrier. According to the images, the STB-loaded carrier moves among the healthy tissues with pH = 7.2 to kidney cancerous tissue and releases STB in this tumor tissue with pH = 5.4 (Scheme 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro studies of STB loaded PD@His.GQD carrier
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve this aim, 0.500 mL of STB solution (CONC = 500 μg mL−1) was added to 1.00 mL of PD@His.GQD carrier, which was suspended in phosphate buffer (PH = 7.2), then was stirred at room temperature and dialyzed with buffer for 24 h to remove unloaded STB. The STB loading on PD@His.GQD drug delivery system was verified by recording the PD@His.GQD primary FI changes at λex = 380 nm. STB loading decreased the carrier fluorescence emission, so at the end of loading and after removing the unloaded drug, the percentage of STB loading efficiency (Ea) was computed via Eq. (2) [17]:
(2)
E a
( % ) =
C 0
-
C 1
C 0
× 100
In this equation, C0 is the drug initial concentration and C1 is the concentration of unloaded STB. According to the synthesized PD@His.GQD carrier structure, it can be concluded that the STB loading procedure is physical adsorption and the STB physisorption occurs into/onto the inner pore of the synthesized polymeric carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro STB release pattern
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the STB release behavior in response to the pH and temperature alterations, the STB.PD@His.GQD was dispersed in two different release media: pH = 7.2 (normal cells) and pH = 5.4 (cancerous cells) at Temp = 37 °C (physiological body temperature) and Temp = 25 °C. For this intention, after loading STB on PD@His.GQD as described above, the solution was placed in two dialysis bags, once with PB buffer of pH = 7.2 and next with acetate buffer of pH = 5.4 then incubated in a shaker at Temp = 37 °C and Temp = 25 °C at different times in the range 1−20 h. The certaine amount of these solutions were collected each time and their FI changes at the λex = 380 nm were monitored to determine the percentage of STB release at different pHs/Temps and times. As mentioned above, STB release at pH = 5.4 raises the carrier fluorescence emission more than pH = 7.2 at Temp = 37 °C. To measure the release percentage at each of the two pH/Temp conditions, at first a standard calibration curve of different STB concentrations was drawn and then the Eq. (3) was used [18]:
(3)
D r u g

( % ) =

C0 and Ct are the primary STB concentration (the loaded drug) and released STB at each time (t).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of whole cancerous mice body via 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain very translucid in vivo images of deep or superficial tumors and tissues, it is indispensable that tracers labelled with radioactive materials such as radioisotopes, in this case, these radio-tracers will be provided with extra detailed information by emitting more radiation. Another important advantage of these radio-tracers is that they can image both soft and bone tissues, so their very high accumulation in one or more tissues indicates that the organ is damaged or cancerous. In this work, to ensure the selectivity and purposeful movement of the STB.PD@His.GQD system towards the kidney cancerous tissue in the in vivo environment, the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radio-carrier was synthesized. Then a certain amount of synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD was injected into the cancerous mice and the in vivo imaging was carried out using SPECT device. The whole cancerous mice body imaging was accomplished at 10 h and 20 h after injection, to evince and prove that synthesized targeting 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD system accumulates in the kidney cancerous tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD in vivo bio-distribution in cancerous mice
ce:section.ce:para:
The bio-distribution is a chart that shows the amount of radio-carrier distribution in all organs, its circulation in the blood and the intensity of radiation emitted from it in each organ. Immediately after the end of in vivo imaging, the cancerous mice was killed and the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD bio-distribution among different its tissues was calculated. For this objective, the tissues specific activity was calculated (due to the presence of 67Ga radioisotope in the radio-carrier), via HPGe detector that is equipped with a sample holder, using Eq. (4) and afterwards the percent of the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD injected dose per gram of these tissues was computed via Eq. (5) [19,20]:

At the end, according to the obtained data, a bio-distribution graph was drawn from 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD in varied cancerous mice tissues (ID/g % to tissues).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization and surface morphology of the carrier
Analysis of His.GQD via XRD, HR-TEM and FTIR
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the His.GQD was synthesized via thermal pyrolysis (incompletely carbonization) procedure. During this process, CA can be converted to graphene hexagonal crystalline structure with a lot of functional groups of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl on the edge of sheets; also, histidine has imidazole ring, carboxylic acid group, and α-amino groups. So the synthesized His.GQD as a biocompatible and selective agent on the PD@His.GQD carrier surface has many functional groups itself. To prove this claim, the surface morphology of His.GQD was investigated by XRD, HR-TEM, and FTIR (Fig. 2 a–d). The synthesized His.GQD was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 2a). The wide-angle XRD pattern, in the range 2θ = 10–80°, presents a single broad high intensity diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23.0°. The observed broad peak is related to the (002) graphene crystalline structure and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms that well affirmed the GQD lattice spacing formation and embedded histidine onto the graphene sheets edge during pyrolysis [21]. Moreover the hexagonal carbon network structure of His.GQD with a sheet spacing of 0.212 nm has been shown via HR-TEM image (Fig. 2b) with a size distribution from 1 to 4 nm, and an average diameter of 2 nm. To identify and determine the various types of His.GQD functional groups, the FTIR analysis was used (Fig. 2c,d). The obtained results of the FTIR spectra analysis proved that in addition to the GQD sheets synthesized from CA, as GQD carbon source, the primary structure of His, as amino-functionalized agent, has not changed much and its main functional groups are clearly evident in the His.GQD FTIR spectra (Fig. 2c,d). According to His.GQD FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2c) the peaks at 3135.6, 3017.9 and 2900.0 cm−1 can be assigned to the -NH2 groups of histidine (Fig. 2d) at 3124.4, 3016.1 and 2872.1 cm−1. The peaks centered at 3453.6, 1716.4 and 1633.4 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration of −OH, CC/CO and COOH of GQD sheets bonds (Fig. 2c). The stretching vibration peaks of NH, amide I, amide III and CN groups of histidine can be proven by the peak centered at 1456.7 and 1413.2 cm−1, and bands at 1344.3 cm−1 and 1344.5 cm−1 represents the CN/C−OH groups of His and His.GQD. Furthermore, the presence of a peak at 1198.6 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration/rotation of CO and C–H of His.GQD. The existence of all these peaks in the His.GQD FTIR spectra proves the preservation of the histidine main structure during the pyrolysis. Thus, the pyrolysis synthetic procedure leads to the formation of carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, amid and amine functional groups on the synthesized His.GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DLS, TEM and FTIR analysis of PD@His.GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Herein the crystal structure, surface morphology, and functional groups of the novel synthesized PD@His.GQDs targeting drug delivery system were investigated. As aforesaid, this targeting carrier was synthesized using the PAA, EDA, and His.GQD via the one-step hydrothermally crosslinking procedure. Therefore, according to the materials used in the synthesis process can be concluded that the PD@His.GQD has numerous functional groups. To affirm this claim, PD@His.GQD was characterized with DLS, TEM, and FTIR analysis (Fig. 3 a–e). Determination of the exact size of the synthesized PD@His.GQD confirms its synthesis in the dot-scale that is extremely momentous in biological usages. To achieve this objective, at first the hydrodynamic diameters of the PD@His.GQD were calculated 35 nm (Fig. 3a) by DLS. For this purpose and the DLS investigation, the optimized amount of PD@His.GQD solution was diluted 10-fold with deionized water and sonicated at temperature of 25 °C to prevent the aggregation of dots, then DLS was performed at scattering angle of 173° and time of 5 s. Then the morphology, size, and synthesis confirmation of the PD@His.GQD were appraised using TEM images of PD and PD@His.GQD (Fig. 3b,c). For this intention, first the TEM image of PD substrate was prepared (Fig. 3b) and as seen in Fig. 3b, these dots are spherical and have a very good distribution without any adhesion to each other. After modifying PDs with His.GQDs, the deformation of the PD substrate from spherical to hexagonal (His.GQD) was observed clearly which proves the modification of PDs and thus the synthesis of PD@His.GQD (Fig. 3c). To obtain TEM images of the polymer dot (PD) and polymer dot modified histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (PD@His.GQD), the samples were prepared by dripping sonicated solutions onto a copper grid and drying at room temperature before imaging. In addition to morphology, the average size of 34 nm that had a good agreement with the obtained DLS average size. Given that movement liberty plays a key role in the DLS measurements, in this work ethylenediamine is used as a crosslinking agent of polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains to form strong polymeric networks and withal these polymeric networks was coated via His.GQDs, so PAA chains do not have any freedom at all. In addition to the mentioned reasons, a very important point is that the TEM and DLS were performed in the solution, confirming the well formation of PD@His.GQD. As mentioned, in the synthesis of this carrier His.GQD was used as selective agent, in order to prove this claim and demonstrate its functional groups on the carrier, the FTIR analysis done (Fig. 3d,e). As it can be seen in the Fig. 3d, the vibration bonds of −OH/NH2 are centered at 3434.7, 3310.1, 3218.8 and 2965.6 cm−1, COOH and CC/CO vibration bonds at 1653.4 and 1700.9 cm−1, stretching vibration peaks of NH, amide I, amide III and CN groups at 1570.8 and 1486.3 cm−1, CN/C−OH and CO stretching vibration bonds at 1391.1 and 1085.2 cm−1. Analyzing the FTIR spectra perfectly corroborate the amide bonds which are relevant to the crosslinking synthesis procedure of PD@His.GQDs and the peaks of His@GQD (Fig. 3e) on that.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quality evaluation of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD via RTLC and HPLC analyses
ce:section.ce:para: A radiolabeled substance is initially pure, but due to the radiation process, it may lose its purity over time. Therefore, after carrier labeling and preparation radiolabeled 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD as an immensely transparent radio-carrier for in vivo imaging in a cancerous mice model, its radiochemical purity should be verified and measured. The Radio Thin Layer Chromatography (RTLC) and HPLC analyses were used to confirm the radiochemical purity, radiolabeled 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD identification and radiolabeling percentage of the synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD (Fig. 4 a–c). RTLC is one of the best ways to determine the chemical purity of synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD and the binding of 67Ga to STB.PD@His.GQD. To perform RTLC analysis, 6.0 μL of the free 67Ga (Fig. 4a) and then 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD (Fig. 4b) were spotted on Whatman No.1 chromatography papers. Then the papers were immersed in water/methanol (v/v = 1:1) as mobile phase. As shown in the Fig. 4a, using the mixture of mentioned solvents, 67Ga has freely moved along the Whatman paper with Rf = 0.78 and 95 % radiochemical purity (Rf = Rate of Flow), while according to the Fig. 4b, the radiolabeled 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD remains at the end of the paper as a single peak with Rf = 0.08 and 95 % radiochemical purity. The HPLC analysis was carried out in a Whatman reversed phase column via eluent solvents of water/acetonitrile (v/v = 3:2) (Fig. 4c). To perform this analysis, the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD elutriates by sodium citrate solution (1.0 M) and aforesaid solvents, in this case, if a little un-labeled 67Ga remains in the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD, it quickly forms a complex with citrate anion, leaves the column and the corresponding peak will appear in the HPLC chromatogram. As shown in Fig. 4c, the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD cannot form a complex with citrate anion (no peak for 67Ga was seen in the HPLC analysis) and just one peak appears at the longer time of 4.6 min that is another testimony to the label of 67Ga to STB.PD@His.GQD. So according to the obtained results the the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radiochemical purity was proved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism investigation and principles of PD@His.GQD fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: To enquire the PD@His.GQD fluorescence properties, the mechanism of producing fluorescence emission from polymeric networks in the process of this targeting carrier synthesis was studied. In this work, the PD@His.GQD was hydrothermally synthesized via crosslinking PAA and EDA as raw materials of PD through non-conjugated polymer system. As known in chemistry and biology, a cross-linking bond links the chains of the primary polymer together and changes the physical properties of the polymer. Hereon, EDA was used as a linker to link polymeric chains (PAA) via single covalent bond (ϭ bond) and synthesized PDs with sp3 hybridization that leads to a decrease in polymeric movements and QY with negligible toxicity. In addition, wrapping the His.GQD in the synthesized polymer dot networks, leads to an increase the solubility, FI/QY, and red-shifted emission to the visible region of the PD@His.GQD due to the His.GQD aromatic carbon rings with sp2 hybridization and π bond. According to the PD@His.GQD FTIR spectrum, the existence of CO functional group proves that fluorescence center in PD is due to the oxidation process of EDA and the existence of NH proves that this phenomenon might be caused by protonation and de-protonation of amines during the hydrothermal synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro optimization of the carrier synthesis situations
Optimization of the PD@His.GQD synthesis condition
ce:section.ce:para: As noted above, this PD@His.GQD targeting carrier was synthesized from the combination of two substances; His.GQD and PD. To form the GQD sheets in the His.GQD, as targeting agent, with high water solubility, the mass ratio of His/CA plays a very momentous role. In fact, the high His/CA mass ratio causes a bit formation of the graphene sheets and significant reduction in the His.GQD water solubility, as CA has water-soluble functional groups hydroxyl and carboxyl. However, His has hydrophobic imidazole functional group, therefore, in order to synthesize water-soluble His.GQD with high FI, the His/CA mass ratio was optimized and His/CA = 1/4 showed the best results (Fig. 5 ). Also, the synthesized PD@His.GQD is a combination of His.GQD, PAA and EDA, in order to achieve the maximum FI/QY, polymeric network, selectivity, and water solubility the mass ratio of His.GQD/PAA/EDA was optimized. In point of fact, because the PAA chains are the source of polymer, EDA is cross-linking agent and His.GQD is the main agent for carrier selective operation, increasing the FI and water solubility, making the optimization of His.GQD/PAA/EDA ratio a very crucial step. By recording the FI of PD@His.GQD synthesized with different ratios of raw materials, with the ratios of 278 μL of His@GQD/100 mg of PAA/170 μL of EDA, the highest FI = 875 of PD@His.GQD via all above mentioned features was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of temperature and time in the PD@His.GQD synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The PD@His.GQD drug carrier was synthesized by hydrothermal method during the incomplete carbonization of raw materials method. The main advantage of incomplete carbonization is the preservation of the original structure and the required raw materials functional groups in the final product. Furthermore, very low temperatures lead to non-formation of polymeric networks and too high temperatures result in breaking of this crosslinked polymer structure and its vibration that all these phenomena cause a significant PD@His.GQD fluorescence intensity reduction. Therefore, in order to synthesize pure PD@His.GQD with high fluorescence intensity, the reaction must take place under controlled conditions and proceed only to the stage of incomplete carbonization and to achieve this goal, it is very important to optimize the autoclave temperature and reaction time. For this purpose, PD@His.GQD carriers were synthesized with different Temp/time conditions and the FI of each of them was recorded. Finally, according to the obtained results the Temp = 200 °C and time = 10 h were the best optimal conditions for the synthesis of the high fluorescence intensity PD@His.GQD under the incomplete carbonization (Fig. 6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on the His.GQD and PD@His.GQD fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of synthesized His.GQD and PD@His.GQD, surface binding-sites and the performance of targeting STB delivery system (STB loading and release) are affected by pH (Fig. 7 a and b). According to Fig. 7a and b, using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl and NaOH solutions, the influence of pH in the range 3–12 on the His.GQD and PD@His.GQD maximum FI was investigated. By heightening the pH in the pH range 3–7, the FI for both these synthesized QDs increased remarkably with pH change and reached a maximum at pH = 7 and then decreased in the pH range of 8−12. This process happens because at lower pH values (pH < 7) and the addition of acidic hydrogen on the His.GQD and PD@His.GQD surface, their functional groups are protonated and changed that this phenomenon leads to a decrease in their fluorescence intensity. Likewise, the hydroxide ion addition at basic range of pH (pH > 7) on the His.GQD and PD@His.GQD surface, cause to change in their functional groups and reduction in fluorescence emission signal intensity [22]. According to the mentioned items, pH = 7 was selected for the next experiments because at this pH the His.GQD and PD@His.GQD surface functional groups are in their main form, so they have the utmost FI and have the superlative potential for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro STB loading conditions study
The STB loading system response based on pH
ce:section.ce:para: As a matter of fact, the body physiological pH profile varies among cellular compartments, and diseased tissues display diverse and lower pH profile than healthy tissues. Thus the manufacturing stimuli-sensitive systems which can sense and monitor the various stimulants, especially pH and temperature, in healthy and diseased tissues, will have sundry medical applications mainly in drug delivery and bio-imaging. In this work, in order to achieve this goal, PD@His.GQD as a pH/Temp sensitive and targeting STB delivery system was synthesized. The capability of PD@His.GQD as a proper carrier for in vitro STB loading was explored via mixing 1.00 mL of PD@His.GQD with 0.500 mL STB (CONC = 500 μg mL−1) in various pH conditions in the range 3–12 and at Temp = 25 °C (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 8 clearly shows that the loading procedure varied significantly with pH changes, the STB loading heightens as the pH rises from 7 to 7.6 considerably and reaches to the maximum loading (maximal fluorescence turn-off) at pH = 7.2 and then lessens as pH increases in the range 8–12. Electrostatic interactions are likely to cause STB loading on the carrier because under pH = 7.2, the -NH groups on the STB surface (pKa = 9.8) are protonated while the PD@His.GQDs surface charge is negative (according to the carboxyl/hydroxyl functional groups in its FTIR spectrum) and this interaction causes pH-dependent drug loading. So according to the obtained results of STB loading at pH = 7.2, the pH of the healthy tissues, the STB.PD@His.GQD carrier can easily move among the healthy tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The STB loading efficiency calculation
In vitro optimized situations of STB release
Checking STB release from STB.PD@His.GQD with pH
ce:section.ce:para: As indicated, the extracellular pH of the tumors is slightly more acidic than blood and healthy tissues. Therefore, a pH-sensitive and targeting drug delivery system can move in the endocytotic pathway (healthy tissues with body physiological pH = 7.2–7.4) and release their loaded drug selectively in the endosome/lysosome environment (tumor tissues with pH = 4.5–6.0), thus the drug side effects are greatly diminished [23]. In this project, a pH-sensitive targeting PD@His.GQD carrier was synthesized, so it can release its loaded STB in the target acidic environment. Responsive STB release system, based on pH changes in the range 3–11, especially in the range 4–5.6 (weakly acidic, tumor endosome/lysosome environment) was appraised at body physiological temperature 37 °C using a dialysis procedure (Fig. 10 ). For this purpose the STB.PD@His.GQD was placed into a dialysis bag with 3 kDa cut-off molecular weight and was dialyzed through diver’s buffers (range = 3–11) in a shaker with controlled temperature of 37 °C. After 20 h and the completion of the STB release process, the optimal pH of the STB release was obtained by drawing changes fluorescence intensity of each of the solutions to examined pH range by luminescence spectrometer at λex = 380 nm. According to Fig. 10, the maximum STB released was seen at the pH of tumor environment 5.4, given that, at this acidic pH both STB and carrier surface charges are protonated (positive), the electrostatic bond of STB.PD@His.GQD is broken and the STB release happens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the Temp/pH sensitive STB release system
ce:section.ce:para: Today, smart drug delivery systems have plentiful usages in the diverse fields of drug delivery because these systems can release drugs at a specific time and place in response to differing factors such as temperature, pH, light and electric or magnetic fields. Among these smart drug delivery systems, most studies have focused on Temp/pH-sensitive systems (stimulants in cancerous cells) which upon reaching to the target cells, they are accumulated inside the cells and release their loaded drug that lead to the invasive behavior of the drug with tumor cells, increasing the drug efficacy with the least side effects.

To prove the synthesis of this Temp/pH sensitive targeting STB delivery system in this project, the STB.PD@His.GQD was dispersed in cancerous tissues pH = 5.4 (Fig. 11 a,b) and healthy tissues pH = 7.2 (Fig. 11c,d) conditions at Temp = 37 °C and Temp = 25 °C and checked out in the time range 1−20 h. Fig. 11 reveal that STB release occurs in two steps: I) a rapid STB release occurs in the first 10 h and then the slow STB release happens at the target site with high release percentage of 97 % until 20 h (Fig. 11a); II) a rapid STB release accomplishes in the first 10 h and a slow release takes place until 20 h but with very low release percentage of 8 % (Fig. 11c). This rapid and explosive drug releases in the first 10 h after reaching the STB.PD@His.GQD to the target tissue, leading to control of cancer cells growth and proliferation, then the slow and sustained STB release until 20 h, results in the complete cure and destruction of cancer cells. The analysis of these results shows that the synthesized carrier can easily move among healthy tissues with the pH = 7.2 and Temp = 37 °C with negligible release percentage (8 %) and after reaching to the cancerous tissue with the pH = 5.4 and Temp = 37 °C, it releases almost all its loaded STB (release percentage of 97 %). So the obtained outcomes prove the excellent capability of the synthesized targeting polymeric drug carrier (PD@His.GQD) as smart drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD bio-distribution
Cancerous mice whole body in vivo SPECT imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 a illustrates that after the injection of ACHN cell lines (kidney cancerous cells) into the right kidney of mice, the size of the cancerous kidney tissue has grown bigger than the size of another healthy kidney tissue (left kidney). This size growth is a desirable indication of the becoming cancerous kidney tissue. After this step, the cancerous mice model were anesthetized with standard amount of ether, then 300 μL of the synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radio-carrier with 40 μg mL−1 of loaded STB concentration at pH = 7.2 was injected into the cancerous mice tail vein. According to the blood pH, this radio-carrier can move easily through the cancerous mice blood and its whole body in vivo imaging was performed 10 and 20 h via a SPECT device (Fig. 12b,c). As it anticipated and according to the obtained in vivo images, due to the selectivity of the synthesized carrier, the most amounts of injected radio-carrier was accumulated in the kidney cancerous tissue and was remained in this tissue leastwise for 20 h.

Fig. 12b, is for imaging after 10 h which given that 67Ga has not yet been completely removed, a more colorful and perspicuous image of the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD radio-carrier can be seen. In Fig. 12c, the imaging is done after 20 h and since 67Ga begins to separate from 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD (according to in vitro data), a fainter image of the cancerous kidney tissue is recorded. In addition, according to the site of 67Ga radioisotope excretion from the body is intestine and also given that after reaching the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD to the target tissue (cancerous kidney), 67Ga is slowly separated from the radio-carrier, the intestinal tissue is well seen in the images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD bio-distribution in cancerous mice
ce:section.ce:para: The bio-distribution of 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD in different cancerous mice tissues was scrutinized immediately after the last 20 -h imaging by strangling the cancerous mice in an environment containing carbon dioxide gas. For this purpose, the various cancerous mice tissues including blood, liver, stomach, muscle, intestine, spleen, feces, heart, cancerous kidney, healthy kidney, skin, lung, bone, skull bone, brain, and joint were rinsed via normal saline and weighed. From killed cancerous mice aorta, the blood sample was taken. Then according to Eqs. (4) and (5), the ID/g percentage was calculated for each of these tissues and a diagram of the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD bio-distribution in the cancerous mice body was drawn. According to Fig. 13 , the maximum bio-distribution of the synthesized 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD after 20 h post-injection was observed in the kidney cancerous tissue and intestine (the tissue of 67Ga excretion) compared to other tissues. These in vivo obtained outcomes from bio-distribution of the radio-carrier were fully consistent with previous in vitro targeting STB release results at pH = 5.4 (Section 3.4.2, release percentage of 97 %). Therefore considering that the in vitro targeting drug release percentage was obtained as 97 % at cancerous tissue pH, it can be concluded that a small amount (8 %) of carrier has been introduced into other tissues (as shown in Fig. 13) during movement in the body with pH = 7.2 until it reaches the cancerous tissue. To accurately compare the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD bio-distribution in the whole body of cancerous mice and in the target tissue, the study of free 67Ga has also been done in a previous work [13]. The obtained high ID/g% data from previous work demonstrated that 67Ga accumulates in the intestine and easily excretes from the body throughout the feces at the end of its biological half-life [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of STB release kinetic
ce:section.ce:para: The main objective of the controlled drug release carriers synthesis is to maintain the drug concentration at a required amount in the target tissues as long as possible, maintain the plasma drug levels and control the speed and timing of drug release [24,25]. In fact, very rapid drug release leads to the fast depletion of the drug in the body, the need for frequent use of drug in short time intervals, and as a result increase its side effects. In contrast, very slow drug release reduces the drug effect in the target area and leads to a great opportunity for the growth and proliferation of damaged cells. Consequently, carriers are synthesized in such a way that the drug is first quick released into cancer cells for an optimal period of time to prevent the duplication of these cells, then a slow release of the drug releases at a constant rate leads to the drug remaining in the cancer cells for a long time and curing them. For this intent, to achieve beneficial and effective treatment, and to determine the best drug release time at the tumor site, the drug release kinetic studies are performed [26]. Since 1986, the biocompatible/biodegradable water-soluble polymeric carriers have been used extensively in the design and synthesis of controlled drug delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In synopsis, in this study, the synthesis of the PD@His.GQD as a new type of biocompatible stimuli-sensitive targeting drug carrier (Temp/pH-sensitive) for in vitro scrutinizing of all STB loading/release chemical circumstances was synthesized. This carrier with superb photostability, high FI/QY, excellent selectivity and large surface area was synthesized through green, cost-effective, and straightforward hydrothermally crosslinking method. A set of meticulous characterizations (DLS, TEM, and FTIR) authenticated the favorable procurement of PD@His.GQDs with average size of 34 nm. To prove the selective STB delivery to the kidney cancerous tissue, the 67Ga-STB.PD@His.GQD as an extremely transparent targeting radio-carrier was introduced for the first time. After affirming the synthesized radio-carrier radiochemical purity via RTLC and HPLC, the in vivo imaging and bio-distribution investigations were done in the whole body of one kidney cancerous mice. According to all obtained results from in vitro and in vivo studies, the PD@His.GQD was loaded with STB at healthy tissue pH = 7.2 and released it at cancerous tissue pH = 5.4, corroborated well with the in vivo images and bio-distribution graph. Finally, in order to predict the STB release mechanism in the in vivo environment, the release kinetic quantitative analysis of the obtained in vitro STB release data was investigated with four mathematical models including Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas. The obtained results were fitted with Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic model that expresses the STB release mechanism from the synthesized PD@His.GQD carrier is controlled by fickian diffusion.

ce:section.ce:para: H.M. Kashani: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. T. Madrakian: Supervisor and Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, and A. Afkhami: advisor of this project and Writing review & editing.
